lede: Jātaka · ชาดก accounts for 1,633 catalogued manuscripts. It clusters in Phrae (27% of the corpus for this genre), ahead of Lampang and Chiang Mai. Nearly all (94%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (88%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (3%). Dated witnesses run 1471–2024 CE (1233 of 1,633 carry a date).

findings.title: The deepest roots in the collection
findings.gloss: The ten earliest-dated manuscripts, reaching back to 1471 CE — the far anchor of the single continuous timeline that runs all the way to today's amulet stalls.

findings.title: The heavyweights: the longest manuscripts
findings.gloss: The twelve longest texts by leaf count, led by one running to 1,046 leaves.

authored.title: Jātaka
authored.body_html:
What this is

Jātaka (Lanna chadok) are the birth-stories: tales of the Buddha's previous lives as the Bodhisatta, working his way through the perfections across some five hundred existences. In the Pali canon they sit in the Khuddaka-nikāya, grouped by the number of verses each tale carries — the nipāta, which surfaces directly in the titles here (Wisati nibat, the twenty-verse group; Panca nibat; Sap tingsa nibat). But the Lanna jātaka corpus is wider than the canon. Its living heart is the paññāsa jātaka, the extra-canonical "fifty births" composed in mainland Southeast Asia, and above all the Mahāchat — the Vessantara, the Great Birth, whose annual recitation (Thet Mahachat) is one of the central preaching festivals of the northern year.

So while this is not a wichaa genre, it is not inert scripture either. These were the preacher's working repertoire, copied to be read aloud; the local verse retellings that fill out the collection (Along this and that) are Lanna literature as much as they are Buddhist canon.

The shape of the collection

Jātaka is one of the largest genres in the collection — over 1,600 witnesses — and, like the canonical texts it sits beside, it is written almost entirely in Tham Lanna (94%) on palm-leaf (88%). Paper barely registers here: a jātaka is a durable sermon-text, copied into the same canonical format as the scripture it accompanies, not a working almanac to be opened flat.

On paper the collection looks like a Phrae–Lampang tradition (Phrae 27%, Lampang 22%), but that shape is deceptive and worth reading carefully — see the first note below.

Notes

  • The place-distribution is a library artifact, not a map of production. A single repository, Wat Sung Men in Phrae, supplies more than a fifth of the entire genre from one temple (over 350 witnesses). Phrae leads because Wat Sung Men's library survived and was catalogued, not because Phrae "wrote more jātaka." Read every province and temple count in this genre against that fact.
  • Canon and apocrypha are collapsed under one label. The nipāta groupings of the canonical five hundred, the extra-canonical paññāsa jātaka, and the Mahāchat all arrive here as plain "Jataka" — including a batch recently reclaimed from a mislabelled "Buddhist Tale." Splitting these apart — canonical nipāta vs. paññāsa vs. Vessantara — is the obvious next taxonomy pass, and the one most useful to a reader.
  • Overlap with recitation. Because the Mahāchat is performed, not just read, expect jātaka to shade into the liturgy and chanting corpus wherever a title names a thet (sermon) or a festival recitation.
